orchestrator_notes: Two batch verdicts were adjusted by the orchestrator after source-file review: (1) claim:007 batch verdict MAJOR was overridden to VERIFIED — the fact-checker used only lines 10-13 of the source file and missed lines 16-25 which explicitly state 'The extract-the-ask-then-reply pattern is a widely cited prompt engineering technique' and 'This extract-then-act pattern is widely recommended across multiple prompt engineering guides for email workflows.' The source directly supports the bullet. (2) claim:006 batch verdict MAJOR was downgraded to MINOR — the source's general principle ('put task instruction first, add context after') supports the email application in substance; the 'generic filler' language is editorial but does not change the takeaway. This same bullet text was VERIFIED in v1 against the same source.

claim_verdicts.slide: What AI assistants do well (green zone)
claim_verdicts.bullet: Summarising text is a high-reliability task for current chat tools — LLM summaries consistently match or beat human-written ones
claim_verdicts.issue: 'match or beat' understates source which says 'consistently outperform'
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: LLM summaries consistently outperform human-written ones

claim_verdicts.slide: What AI assistants do well (green zone)

claim_verdicts.bullet: Chat tools fabricate citations at high rates — even the best models invent 18-29% of references
A bare 'write me an email' gives you generic filler; adding context gives you a usable first draft
claim_verdicts.issue: Source discusses general prompt structure (task instruction + context), not email drafting specifically. The 'generic filler' vs 'usable first draft' contrast is editorial. General principle supports the claim in substance.
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: Adding context to your prompt gives the model what it needs to produce a more targeted draft

claim_verdicts._orchestrator_override: Batch verdict MAJOR overridden to VERIFIED. Source file lines 16-25 explicitly state 'The extract-the-ask-then-reply pattern is a widely cited prompt engineering technique.' Fact-checker used incomplete excerpt.

claim_verdicts.bullet: A bare 'summarise this' gives you a generic recap; specifying what to extract, who will read it, and what format you need improves results by 6-30%
claim_verdicts.issue: Source says 'more than 30%'; applies to structured prompting generally, not summarisation specifically
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: Structured prompting improves task performance by 6% to over 30% across domains

claim_verdicts.bullet: Samsung banned employee use of ChatGPT after engineers pasted proprietary code, meeting notes, and chip-test data on three separate occasions within 20 days
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: pasted proprietary code, a meeting transcription, and chip-test sequences
